title: Why Cooking and Cleaning Can Save You During Kundalini Crisis
meta_description: You're here, aren't you? It's probably 3 AM, and you're staring at the ceiling, feeling like your brain's on fire and your body's a live wire. Or maybe you
body:
Why Cooking and Cleaning Can Save You During Kundalini Crisis

You're here, aren't you? It's probably 3 AM, and you're staring at the ceiling, feeling like your brain's on fire and your body's a live wire. Or maybe you're curled up, shaking, convinced you're losing your mind. I know that feeling. I've been there. The terror, the isolation, the sheer, unadulterated madness of it all. They call it a "kundalini awakening," but when you're in the thick of it, it feels more like a kundalini crisis. It's not the blissful, enlightened state they talk about in books. It's raw, it's terrifying, and it can break you. But here's the thing: you can get through this. And sometimes, the very things you're probably neglecting right now - cooking and cleaning - can be your anchors.

Look, when your entire being is being rewired, when energy's coursing through you in ways you never thought possible, your mind screams for something, anything, familiar. Something solid to hold onto. You're probably feeling disconnected from your body, from reality, like you're floating somewhere outside yourself. That's a classic symptom. It's why gurus tell you to ground yourself. But what does that even mean when your nervous system feels like it's short-circuiting? It means coming back to the basics. It means doing things that force you back into your physical body, into the present moment, into the tangible world around you.

I remember nights where I felt like I was dissolving. My identity, my sense of self - just gone. I couldn't sleep, couldn't eat, couldn't think straight. My apartment became a disaster zone because I couldn't even manage to pick up a sock. That's a dangerous spiral, you know? The outer chaos reflects the inner chaos, and it just makes everything worse. You need to interrupt that pattern. You need to create some order, even if it's just a tiny bit, to remind yourself that you still exist, that you can still exert some control over your immediate environment. It's not about achieving enlightenment through scrubbing a toilet, believe me. It's about survival. It's about giving your overstimulated system something simple, repetitive, and real to focus on. It's about tricking your brain into remembering it's connected to a body, and that body lives in a physical space. Does that land?

The Power of the Mundane: Why It Works

So why these two specific activities? Why cooking and cleaning? It's not arbitrary. They're not just chores; they're acts of creation and restoration. They demand your attention in a very specific, physical way. They're sensory. And when you're feeling completely overwhelmed by internal sensations, external, physical sensations can be a godsend. They pull you out of your head, out of the swirling vortex of energy and fear, and into the here and now.

Sensory Engagement: When you're cooking, you're chopping, smelling, tasting, feeling textures. When you're cleaning, you're scrubbing, seeing dirt disappear, feeling the warmth of water, smelling cleaning products (or natural scents if you're like me and prefer less chemicals). These aren't abstract thoughts; they're concrete experiences. Your hands are busy, your eyes are focused, your nose is engaged. It's a full-body experience, even if it's a simple one. This is key when your internal world is so loud it drowns everything else out. It's like a gentle reset button for your nervous system. It's a way to tell your brain, "Hey, we're still in a body. We're still in this room. Everything is okay, for now."

Repetitive Motion: Think about washing dishes, folding laundry, sweeping the floor, chopping vegetables. These are all repetitive, almost meditative actions. They don't require complex decision-making, which is exactly what you can't do when your mind is fragmented. The rhythm of these actions can be incredibly soothing. It's like a lullaby for an overactive nervous system. It creates a predictable flow, a small pocket of order in what feels like utter chaos. I'll tell you what, sometimes just the repetitive motion of wiping down a counter was enough to bring me back from the brink. It was a tiny, achievable victory.

Tangible Results: You can see the clean counter. You can smell the food cooking. You can taste the meal you made. These are immediate, undeniable results. When you feel like you're achieving nothing, accomplishing nothing, and everything is falling apart, these small, tangible successes are incredibly important. They give you a sense of agency, a reminder that you can still make an impact on your environment, even if it's just making your bed. That feeling of accomplishment, no matter how small, sends a message to your brain: "You're not completely powerless. You can still do things." This helps combat the overwhelming sense of helplessness that often accompanies a kundalini crisis.

Cooking: Nourishment for Body and Soul

Let's talk about cooking specifically. You're probably not eating properly, are you? When you're in crisis, appetite often goes out the window. Or you're just grabbing whatever's easiest, which usually isn't nourishing. But your body, especially now, needs good fuel. It's going through a massive energetic transformation. It needs vitamins, minerals, real food. And making that food yourself? That's a powerful act of self-care.

When you cook, you're actively engaging with the elements. You're transforming raw ingredients into something edible, something that will sustain you. It's a primal act. And honestly, it's a way to show yourself love when you probably feel completely unlovable, completely broken. You're creating something for yourself. You're literally putting energy into your own nourishment. Don't underestimate the psychological impact of that. It's not just about the food; it's about the intention behind it.

Start small, you know? Don't try to make a gourmet meal. Boil some pasta. Scramble some eggs. Make a simple soup. The act of preparing it, even if it's minimal, is what matters. The smell of garlic sizzling, the sight of fresh vegetables - these are grounding sensory inputs. They pull you into the present. They remind you of the physical world, the one your mind is trying to escape. And then, eating the food you've made? That's another layer of grounding. You're feeding your body, literally connecting with it through the act of digestion. It's a full circle of self-care that can be incredibly stabilizing.

Cleaning: Creating a Sanctuary

And then there's cleaning. Your environment reflects your internal state, and vice versa. If your space is chaotic, it's going to feed into your internal chaos. You might not have the energy to clean your whole house, and that's okay. Don't even try. Just pick one small area. Your bedside table. A corner of your room. The kitchen sink. Just one thing.

The act of cleaning, of bringing order to disorder, is incredibly symbolic. You're literally clearing away stagnation, making space. It's like you're telling your nervous system, "We're cleaning house here. We're making room for something new, something better." It's a physical manifestation of trying to get your internal house in order. And the feeling of moving things, wiping things down, seeing the dust disappear - that's a tangible victory. It's a clear before-and-after. And when you're feeling lost in the endlessness of kundalini symptoms, those small, clear victories are vital.

Think about it: a clean space feels different, doesn't it? It feels lighter, calmer. It's easier to breathe, easier to just be. When your internal world is a storm, having a calm, clean external space can be a lifeline. It becomes a sanctuary, a place where you can retreat and feel a tiny bit of peace. You're creating that sanctuary for yourself, with your own hands. That's powerful. That's self-respect. That's telling yourself, "I deserve a clean, safe space." Even if you don't feel like you deserve it, do it anyway. The act itself will start to shift something within you.

Practical Steps to Ground Yourself

Okay, so you're probably thinking, "Easier said than done, Kalesh. I can barely get out of bed." And you're right. I know. But here's the thing: you don't need to do it perfectly. You just need to do something. Even if it's for five minutes. Even if it's just once a day. Small, consistent actions are what build momentum.

Start Small, Start Now

  • Pick ONE thing: Don't try to clean your entire kitchen. Just wash one dish. Or wipe down one counter. Or make your bed. Seriously, just one thing.
  • Set a timer: Five minutes. That's it. Can you clean for five minutes? Can you chop vegetables for five minutes? You'd be surprised what you can get done in five focused minutes. And if you feel like doing more after five minutes, great! If not, you've still accomplished something.
  • Focus on the sensations: As you're doing it, really pay attention. Feel the water on your hands. Smell the soap. Hear the scrubbing. See the dirt disappear. This is the grounding part. This is what pulls you out of your head.
  • No judgment: This isn't about perfection. It's about participation. If you're having a panic attack while washing a dish, that's okay. Just keep washing the dish. The act itself is the medicine.

I remember one night, I felt like I was going to float right out of my body. My anxiety was through the roof. I forced myself to go to the kitchen and just wash the few dishes in the sink. It took maybe ten minutes. But by the end of it, my breathing had slowed, my focus had returned, and I felt a tiny bit more tethered to reality. It was a small miracle, honestly. So yeah, don't discount the power of the mundane.

Another thing that helped me with grounding, especially at night when the energy was wild, was physical pressure. I'm talking about weighted blankets. I know it sounds simple, but that deep pressure can be incredibly calming to an overstimulated nervous system. It's like a constant, gentle hug. If you can get your hands on one, I highly recommend it. I used this one for a long time: YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs - Heavy Blanket for Adults (paid link). It made a noticeable difference in my ability to even just lie still.

Connecting to Earth: Beyond the House

Once you start getting a handle on your immediate environment, you can expand this concept of grounding. You're not just cleaning your house; you're cleaning your connection to the Earth. You're literally trying to connect your energy to something stable and vast. This is where "earthing" comes in. It sounds a bit woo-woo, I know, but it's just about connecting your body directly to the Earth's electrical energy. Walking barefoot on grass or dirt, for instance. But when you're in crisis, that might feel impossible or even unsafe if you're feeling really disoriented.

That's where earthing products can help. They're designed to bring that connection indoors. I found them really helpful when I was too unstable to go outside or when the weather was bad. They won't solve everything, but they can definitely help calm the nervous system and provide a subtle, consistent grounding. I used a few different things, depending on what felt right. For sleeping, something like this was great: Barefoot Restoration Grounding Sheet Set (paid link). Or if you're just sitting at your desk, trying to stay present, an earthing mat can be really useful: Grounding Mat for Desk - Earthing Mat for Better Sleep (paid link). And for general use, a half sheet can be placed under your feet or wherever you're sitting: Earthing Half Sheet with Grounding Connection Cord (paid link). They're not magic bullets, but they're tools. And when you're in this state, you need all the tools you can get.

The whole point of these activities - cooking, cleaning, earthing - isn't to make your kundalini go away. It's to help you manage the crisis, to give you some stability, to remind your body and mind that you're still here, still present, still capable of simple actions. It's about surviving the storm until you can find your footing again. It's about bringing your energy down, out of the chaotic upper centers, and back into your body, into the Earth.

You're Not Alone in This

I know it feels like you're the only one. That no one could possibly understand this hell you're going through. But you're not. There are more of us than you think. And we've all found our own ways to cope. For me, it was often the most basic, seemingly insignificant actions that made the biggest difference. The grand spiritual practices felt impossible. The simple, physical ones? They were my lifeline.

So please, give yourself a break. Don't beat yourself up for not being able to meditate for hours or for not feeling blissful. Just focus on the next small thing you can do. Can you wash one spoon? Can you wipe down one square foot of counter? Can you make yourself a simple sandwich? That's enough. That's more than enough. You're doing something. You're fighting. And that's all that matters right now.

This isn't forever, even though it feels like it right now. This crisis will pass. You will find stability again. And these mundane acts of self-care, of engaging with the physical world, they're not just chores. They're acts of resilience. They're acts of self-preservation. They're how you start to rebuild, one small, intentional movement at a time. Hang in there. You've got this, even if it doesn't feel like it. Just try one small thing. Right?

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
